content.text: Dependency Upgrade Cadence and Review Rules

Unmanaged dependency upgrades are a leading cause of production regressions in GenAI services. Provider SDK minor versions can silently change tool-calling JSON schemas, response object shapes, or streaming chunk formats. Orchestration framework patch releases have broken prompt template rendering with no documented migration path. An upgrade cadence policy sets explicit rules for how often dependencies are reviewed, what review steps each tier requires, and what the rollback SLA is when an upgrade causes a regression.

The cadence rules also prevent the opposite failure mode: indefinite deferrals that leave services pinned to versions with known security issues. Teams need a schedule that is predictable enough to plan around but strict enough that no dependency goes unreviewed for more than a quarter.

attrs.alt: Dependency Upgrade Cadence by Tier and Severity

content.text: Core Cadence Rules by Tier
Upgrade frequency and review requirements scale with dependency tier. Higher-tier packages carry more architectural risk and require proportionally more review effort per upgrade.
Tier 1 — Core Provider SDKs (openai, anthropic, boto3, google-generativeai)

Patch and minor upgrades are reviewed monthly by the Platform team. The team maintains a version watch using Dependabot or Renovate, configured to create PRs automatically for patch upgrades within the approved version range. Automated PRs merge without a manual RFC if all of the following pass:
The CI allowlist gate confirms the new version is within the approved range (e.g.
The full eval suite passes with the new SDK version.
The security scanner finds no new HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs in the new version.
The build and smoke tests pass.
Major version upgrades (e.g.
openai 1.x → 2.x

) require an architecture review to assess breaking changes in the API client interface, response models, and any streaming or async behaviour. The review is filed as an RFC update (not a new RFC) since Tier 1 packages are pre-approved as a class.
Tier 2 — Orchestration Frameworks (langchain-core, llama-index, semantic-kernel)

Patch upgrades are batched monthly and reviewed by the team that owns the relevant service. The review gate is the eval suite: if evals pass with the new version, the upgrade merges. Because these packages are pinned to exact versions, the allowlist must be updated as part of the PR.

Minor upgrades require an architecture re-review since orchestration framework minor versions routinely change the chain execution model, tool-calling interface, or memory abstraction. The original RFC is updated with a new section documenting what changed and confirming the adapter interface still holds. Legal re-review is not required unless the license changed.
Major upgrades require a full RFC re-review — the same process as the original approval — plus a migration plan that specifies:
Which adapter methods change and how they are updated.
Whether the upgrade can be done in a single PR or requires a compatibility shim period.
The target date for removing any compatibility shim.
Tier 3 — Tooling and Helpers (tiktoken, instructor, pydantic-ai)
Quarterly cadence: the Platform team reviews all Tier 3 packages once per quarter. Each review covers:
CVE status (any new findings since the last review?).
Maintenance health (last release, open issue count, maintainer activity).
License change (some projects have changed from MIT to SSPL or BSL between versions).
Whether the package is still needed (packages that no longer serve a purpose should be removed rather than upgraded).

Patch upgrades discovered during the quarterly review follow the same auto-merge path as Tier 1 patch upgrades if all CI gates pass. Minor and major upgrades require a new RFC section and security + legal sign-off.
Automating the Upgrade Pipeline
Manual dependency upgrades are error-prone and inconsistently applied. The recommended automation stack uses Renovate (or Dependabot) configured with tier-specific rules:
The
requiredStatusChecks

for Tier 1 auto-merges must include the eval suite — this is the most important guard against silent regressions. A provider SDK patch that changes response serialisation will cause eval failures before it can merge.
The Upgrade PR Checklist
Every non-auto-merge upgrade PR must include a checklist in the PR description. This standardises review and prevents shortcuts:

## Dependency Upgrade Checklist

**Package:** `langchain-core 0.3.15 → 0.3.22`
**Tier:** 2
**Upgrade type:** Patch

### Gates
- [ ] Allowlist updated in `.approved-dependencies/allowlist.json`
- [ ] `requirements.txt` pinned to exact new version
- [ ] Eval suite passed (link to CI run):
- [ ] Security scan: no new HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs
- [ ] License unchanged (MIT → MIT)

### Impact Assessment
- [ ] Checked CHANGELOG for breaking changes (link):
- [ ] Adapter interface unaffected (or adapter updated — describe):
- [ ] Prompt templates render correctly (verified in staging):

### Rollback Plan
- Revert this PR; pin back to `langchain-core==0.3.15`
- Rollback SLA: 8 hours from regression detection
Eval Suite as the Upgrade Gate

The evaluation suite is the primary quality signal for dependency upgrades, not just unit tests. Unit tests verify that code calls the right methods; evals verify that the model produces correct outputs with the upgraded library. This distinction matters because provider SDK upgrades can change the shape of the messages sent to the model, the formatting of tool-call results, or the handling of streaming chunks — all of which affect output quality without raising any Python exceptions.
The eval gate for dependency upgrades must run a representative sample of the production eval suite, not just the fast smoke tests. Configure CI to run the full eval suite on any PR labelled
dep-upgrade

# .github/workflows/eval-gate.yml (excerpt)
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [labeled]

jobs:
  run-evals:
    if: contains(github.event.label.name, 'dep-upgrade')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install with candidate versions
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run full eval suite
        run: pytest evals/ -v --timeout=300
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}

      - name: Fail PR if any eval degrades by >2%
        run: python scripts/compare_eval_scores.py           --baseline evals/baseline-scores.json           --current evals/results/latest.json           --threshold 0.02
The
compare_eval_scores.py

script compares each metric in the current run against the stored baseline. A regression of more than 2% on any tracked metric blocks the PR. This threshold prevents false-blocks from natural variation while catching genuine regressions.
Handling Upgrade Freezes
Upgrade freezes are planned periods during which no non-security dependency upgrades are merged. Common freeze windows include:
Pre-release freeze (2 weeks before a major release).
Prevents late dependency regressions from delaying the release.
Holiday/on-call reduced staffing windows.
Avoids upgrades when the team cannot respond quickly to a rollback.
Major infrastructure migration.
When the service is simultaneously being migrated to a new cloud region or runtime, dependency churn increases diagnosis complexity.
Freeze windows are declared in the repository by adding a
freeze.json
file to
.approved-dependencies/

The Renovate/Dependabot schedule configuration reads this file and suppresses non-security PRs during the freeze window. Security patches with CRITICAL severity bypass the freeze automatically; HIGH-severity patches require CISO sign-off to merge during a freeze.
Tracking Upgrade Debt

Dependencies that have fallen behind their approved version range accumulate upgrade debt. Left unmanaged, upgrade debt compounds: a package that is four minor versions behind requires a larger migration than one that is one version behind, and the eval delta is harder to attribute.
A weekly CI job produces an upgrade debt report by comparing the current pinned versions against the latest approved versions:

# scripts/upgrade_debt_report.py
import json, subprocess
from datetime import date
from pathlib import Path

allowlist = json.loads(Path(".approved-dependencies/allowlist.json").read_text())["approved"]
result = subprocess.run(
    ["pip", "list", "--format=json", "--outdated"],
    capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
)
outdated = {p["name"].lower(): p for p in json.loads(result.stdout)}

print(f"Upgrade Debt Report — {date.today()}\n{'='*60}")
for entry in allowlist:
    name = entry["name"]
    tier = entry["tier"]
    if name in outdated:
        pkg = outdated[name]
        print(f"  Tier {tier}  {name:35s}  current={pkg['version']:12s}  latest={pkg['latest_version']}")
The report is posted as a comment on the monthly dependency review issue, giving the team a clear picture of accumulated drift before the review meeting.
Regression Detection After an Upgrade

Even with passing CI evals, some regressions only surface under production load or with specific user inputs that are not represented in the eval suite. The upgrade cadence policy must pair with a post-deploy regression detection window.
Canary Deployment for Tier 2 and Tier 3 Upgrades
After merging a Tier 2 or Tier 3 upgrade, deploy to a canary slice (5–10% of production traffic) and hold for a soak period before full rollout. During the soak period, monitor three signals:
Signal
What to Watch
Regression Threshold
Eval score in production
Sample 200 production requests through an online evaluator that scores relevance and correctness
Drop of >2% from pre-upgrade baseline triggers rollback
Error rate
4xx and 5xx responses from the LLM integration layer
Any sustained increase of >0.5% over baseline
p99 latency
End-to-end response time at the 99th percentile
Increase of >20% from baseline sustained for >10 minutes
Tool-call parse failures
Structured output parsing errors when the SDK deserialises tool-call responses
Any non-zero rate in production (was zero pre-upgrade)

The soak period is 24 hours for minor upgrades and 48 hours for major upgrades. If no regression signals fire during the soak, the canary is promoted to 100%. If any signal fires, the canary is rolled back and the upgrade PR is reverted.
Rollback Procedure
Rollback for dependency upgrades is a pin-revert, not a feature-flag toggle. The procedure:
Revert the upgrade PR in GitHub (one-click revert button creates a new PR).
Merge the revert PR — this restores the previous pin in
requirements.txt
and the previous allowlist entry.
Redeploy from the reverted commit — the CI pipeline automatically rebuilds and promotes through environments.
File a regression report in the upgrade tracking issue: what signal fired, what input pattern triggered it, and what the root cause is suspected to be.
Do not re-attempt the upgrade until the root cause is understood and a fix (either in the library or in the service's integration code) is in place.

The rollback SLA starts from the moment the regression signal fires, not from when a human notices it. Configure automated alerting so that on-call is paged when any of the four signals above exceed their threshold — relying on manual dashboard checks is not compatible with a 4-hour rollback SLA.
Coordinating Cross-Service Upgrades

In a microservices GenAI platform, multiple services may share the same Tier 2 or Tier 3 package. Upgrading one service at a time to the same new version is safe but creates a temporary inconsistency in the allowlist: if the allowlist records the new exact version, services still on the old version will fail their allowlist check.
The recommended coordination pattern:
RFC approved first.
The new version is added to the allowlist as an approved alternative entry before any service upgrades.
Parallel upgrade PRs.
Each service creates an upgrade PR that pins to the new version. PRs can be opened simultaneously.
Staged merge.
Merge service PRs in order of risk — start with the service with the lowest traffic or best eval coverage. If the first service passes its soak window, proceed to the next.
Old version removed from allowlist.
Once all services have migrated, a cleanup PR removes the old version from the allowlist. Services that missed the migration are now blocked by CI until they upgrade.

The allowlist cleanup PR is important — without it, the old version remains approved indefinitely and services never feel the pressure to migrate. Set a migration deadline (typically 30 days from the RFC approval date) and track it on the upgrade issue.
Provider SDK Breaking-Change Patterns

Knowing which change patterns have historically caused regressions in LLM SDK upgrades helps teams focus their eval coverage appropriately. The following patterns appear repeatedly across major provider SDK versions:
Change Pattern
Example
Eval Coverage Required
Tool-call response schema change
openai 0.28→1.0: function_call replaced by tool_calls array
All evals that use tools or function calling
Streaming chunk format change
Chunk object moved from choices[0].delta.content to choices[0].message.content in some intermediate versions
Any eval that uses streaming responses and assembles chunks
Model name format change
gpt-4 → gpt-4-turbo → gpt-4o — different tokenisation and context limits
All evals; compare output quality, not just format
Async interface change
Some SDKs moved from AsyncOpenAI class to async methods on base client
Any service that uses async prompt chains or parallel calls
Retry and timeout defaults
Default retry count, backoff, and timeout changed between versions
Load evals; measure p99 latency and error rate under concurrent requests
Pydantic version pin conflict
Several provider SDKs pin Pydantic v1 while instructor/pydantic-ai require v2
Any eval that uses structured output extraction

Each of these patterns has caused production incidents. Build targeted regression evals that exercise the relevant interface — tool-calling evals, streaming assembly evals, async concurrency evals — and run them as required gates on every SDK upgrade, not just as part of the general eval suite.
Communicating Upgrade Windows to Dependents

When a service that provides a shared prompt API or embedding endpoint upgrades its LLM SDK, downstream services that call it may see different output formats or quality characteristics. This is especially true when upgrading orchestration framework versions that change how chains are executed.
Before merging a major upgrade, notify downstream service owners through:
Upgrade notice in the shared Slack channel.
Three days before the planned merge: "Service X will upgrade langchain-core from 0.3.15 to 0.4.0 on [date]. If you consume Service X's
/api/generate
endpoint, please review the migration guide and test against staging."
Changelog in the service's API documentation.
Document any output format changes (e.g. citations now returned in a structured list rather than inline text) in the service's CHANGELOG.md and API docs.
Staging environment advanced availability.
Deploy the upgraded service to staging five days before production to give downstream teams time to test integration.

Failure to communicate cross-service upgrade windows is the most common cause of incident escalations during dependency upgrades in multi-service GenAI platforms. The service doing the upgrading rarely experiences a visible incident — the incident surfaces in a downstream service that wasn't prepared for the output format change.
Measuring and Reporting Upgrade Programme Health
An upgrade cadence policy is only valuable if it is actually followed. Track programme health with four metrics, reviewed monthly in the team's engineering health dashboard:
Metric
Formula
Target
Action if Breached
Mean time to patch (MTTP)
Average days from CVE publication to patch deployed in production
≤7 days for HIGH, ≤2 days for CRITICAL
Review triage process; add on-call escalation path
Upgrade debt ratio
Packages behind approved version / total approved packages
Schedule dedicated upgrade sprint; block new feature work if >25%
Auto-merge success rate
Tier 1 patch auto-merges that passed all gates / total attempted
Review eval suite coverage; investigate flaky evals
Rollback frequency
Dependency-upgrade-related rollbacks per quarter
<2 per quarter
Review eval suite quality for the regressed package tier; tighten soak window
Post-incident reviews for dependency-upgrade regressions follow the same blameless format as other production incidents, but with two additional questions specific to the cadence policy:
Was this upgrade on the scheduled cadence, or was it expedited?

Expedited upgrades (CVE patches, urgent feature requirements) bypass the standard eval gate window. If the regression came from an expedited upgrade, review whether the expedited path has adequate safeguards.
Which eval should have caught this and did not?
Every regression is a gap in eval coverage. File a backlog item to add the missing eval case before closing the incident review.

The rollback frequency metric deserves particular attention. Two or more rollbacks per quarter indicates that either the eval suite is insufficient for the packages being upgraded, or the soak window is too short to expose production-specific failure modes. Both are solvable: extend soak windows for Tier 2 upgrades, add production-representative load evals, or improve canary traffic routing to cover edge-case user inputs.
Version Pinning Strategies and Trade-offs
How tightly to pin a dependency is a recurring decision point. Three strategies exist, each with different upgrade effort and stability trade-offs:
Strategy
Syntax (pip)
Behaviour
When to Use
Exact pin
langchain-core==0.3.15
Only this exact version installs. No automatic updates. Any version change requires a PR.
Tier 2 and Tier 3 in production. Maximum stability and auditability.
Compatible release
langchain-core~=0.3.15
Installs 0.3.15 or any 0.3.x above it, but not 0.4.0. Pip resolves the latest compatible patch.
Not recommended for LLM packages — patch versions of orchestration frameworks have caused regressions.
Minimum + ceiling
openai>=1.30,<2
Installs 1.30 or any 1.x above it, but not 2.0. Allows all minor and patch updates within the major.
Tier 1 provider SDKs only, where the vendor maintains strict semver and the platform team reviews minor changes monthly.

The practical recommendation for production GenAI services: use exact pins for all Tier 2 and Tier 3 packages; use minimum-plus-ceiling only for Tier 1 packages where the provider has a proven track record of semver discipline and the platform team's monthly review catches breaking changes before they affect downstream services.
Avoid compatible-release pins (
) for any package in the LLM ecosystem. Orchestration frameworks and helper libraries do not consistently follow semver — minor and patch releases have introduced breaking changes in
langchain-core
llama-index
, and
instructor
. The safety guarantee that
implies simply does not hold for this package class.
Lock files (
pip-compile
output or
uv.lock

) record the full transitive dependency graph at a point in time. Always commit lock files to the repository. When reproducing a bug, reinstall from the lock file to guarantee the same dependency versions that were running in production.
Summary

A structured upgrade cadence policy prevents both upgrade-induced regressions and indefinite version pinning. Tier 1 provider SDKs support monthly auto-merge of patch upgrades once CI eval gates pass; orchestration and tooling packages follow monthly and quarterly batch reviews with architecture and legal sign-off scaled to the upgrade magnitude. Renovate configuration encodes the tier-specific rules so upgrades arrive as labelled PRs on a predictable schedule. The eval suite is the critical gate: a dependency upgrade that silently degrades output quality must be caught before it reaches production, not after. Freeze windows, upgrade debt tracking, and rollback SLAs complete the policy, ensuring the team always knows the state of every approved dependency.
